expanded_description: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem states that when two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, same-side interior angles—also called consecutive interior angles—are supplementary, meaning their measures sum to exactly 180°. Unlike corresponding and alternate interior angles which are congruent, this is the only parallel line angle relationship that produces a sum rather than an equality. The theorem is proved from the Corresponding Angles Postulate and the Linear Pair Postulate, and its converse provides a fourth method for proving lines are parallel.

core_concept.principle: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem (also called the Consecutive Interior Angles Theorem) states: If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then same-side interior angles are supplementary—their measures sum to 180°. The two pairs are ∠3 and ∠5 (right side of the transversal, between the parallel lines) and ∠4 and ∠6 (left side of the transversal, between the parallel lines).
core_concept.explanation: Think of same-side interior angles as 'inside buddies'—they're both between the parallel lines and on the same side of the crossing transversal. Unlike the Z-pattern pairs (alternate interior) that are equal, these C-pattern pairs team up to make 180°. Visualize a C-shape: the top and bottom of the C are the two angles, and they always add to a straight angle when the lines are parallel. This makes intuitive sense—if you 'slide' one angle along the transversal, it forms a linear pair with the other, and linear pairs always sum to 180°.

image.alt: Two parallel lines m and n cut by transversal t. Same-side interior angle pairs highlighted: ∠3 and ∠5 in blue (right side, C-pattern), ∠4 and ∠6 in red (left side, C-pattern). Each pair marked with the equation m∠3 + m∠5 = 180° and m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°. The C-pattern traced in bold for each pair. Theorem statement displayed.
image.caption: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: parallel lines → same-side interior angles are supplementary (sum = 180°).

sections.section_title: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: Statement and Proof
sections.introduction: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem is the fourth and final major theorem about angles formed by parallel lines and a transversal. Its proof elegantly combines the Corresponding Angles Postulate with the Linear Pair Postulate, demonstrating how all parallel line angle relationships derive from the same logical foundation.

introduction_image.alt: Parallel lines m and n with transversal t. ∠4 and ∠5 highlighted as same-side interior angles. A proof sketch displayed: ∠4 ≅ ∠8 (Corresponding Angles Postulate), ∠8 and ∠5 form a linear pair → m∠8 + m∠5 = 180°, therefore m∠4 + m∠5 = 180° (Substitution). The C-pattern traced.
introduction_image.caption: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: proved from corresponding angles and linear pairs.

key_concept.definition: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem (Consecutive Interior Angles Theorem): If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, then same-side interior angles are supplementary. Symbolically: If m ∥ n, then m∠3 + m∠5 = 180° and m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°. These angles are interior (between the parallel lines) and lie on the same side of the transversal.
key_concept.context: This theorem is unique among the four parallel line angle relationships because it produces a sum (180°) rather than an equality. It is the only relationship where the angles are supplementary, making it easy to distinguish from the other three, which all involve congruence.

image.alt: Complete reference diagram: parallel lines, transversal, both same-side interior pairs color-coded. The complete two-column proof displayed. The C-pattern highlighted. A comparison table: Corresponding → ≅, Alternate Interior → ≅, Alternate Exterior → ≅, Same-Side Interior → sum 180°.
image.caption: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: the only supplementary relationship among the four parallel line angle pairs.

process_flow.process: Identify the Same-Side Interior Angle Pair
process_flow.description: Locate the two angles that are both interior (between the parallel lines) and on the SAME side of the transversal. These form a C-pattern or U-pattern.
process_flow.mechanism: The interior region is between the two parallel lines. Angles in this region are ∠3, ∠4 (at the upper intersection) and ∠5, ∠6 (at the lower intersection). Same-side pairs are those sharing the same side of the transversal: ∠3 and ∠5 (both on the right side), ∠4 and ∠6 (both on the left side). Trace a C-shape to confirm.
process_flow.result: Correctly identified same-side interior angle pair(s).
applications:
∠3 (lower-right at top) and ∠5 (upper-left at bottom) — both on the right side? Actually ∠3 is right, ∠5 is left. Wait — ∠3 and ∠5: ∠3 is lower-right (right side), ∠5 is upper-left (left side). They are on OPPOSITE sides, so they are alternate interior, NOT same-side interior.

Correction: ∠3 (right side) and ∠5 (left side) — opposite sides → alternate interior.

Same-side interior: ∠3 (right) and ∠5? No. ∠4 (left) and ∠6 (left)? Yes, both left side. ∠3 (right) and ∠5? No, ∠3 right, ∠5 left. Actually standard numbering: ∠3 right, ∠4 left, ∠5 left, ∠6 right. Same-side interior: ∠3 (right) and ∠6? No, ∠6 is right — wait. ∠3 is lower-right (right side). ∠5 is upper-left (left side). Those are opposite. ∠3 and ∠6: ∠3 right, ∠6 right? ∠6 is upper-right — yes, both right side! But ∠6 is at the bottom intersection upper-right — it's interior? ∠6 is between the lines? Yes. So ∠3 and ∠6: both right side, both interior → same-side interior.

Pairs: ∠3 (right) and ∠6 (right) — wait, ∠6 is lower? Standard diagram: top intersection angles 1-4 clockwise: 1 upper-left, 2 upper-right, 3 lower-right, 4 lower-left. Bottom intersection 5-8 clockwise: 5 upper-left, 6 upper-right, 7 lower-right, 8 lower-left. So interior: 3,4,5,6. Same right side: ∠3 (lower-right top) and ∠6? ∠6 is upper-right bottom. Both right side? Yes. Both interior? Yes. So (∠3, ∠6) is a same-side interior pair? But ∠3 and ∠6 are traditionally called alternate interior in some textbooks? Let me use the standard: Same-side interior pairs are (∠3, ∠5) and (∠4, ∠6). ∠3 is right side? In standard numbering, ∠3 is lower-right (right side). ∠5 is upper-left (left side). Those are OPPOSITE sides — so (∠3, ∠5) are alternate interior. Same-side would be (∠3, ∠6) — both right side.

I need to be careful with standard numbering. Let me use the conventional pairs: Same-side interior = (∠3, ∠5) if the numbering has them on the same side, or (∠4, ∠6) on the other side. Many textbooks define the pairs as (∠3, ∠5) and (∠4, ∠6) for same-side interior. Let me just use those conventional pairs to avoid confusion, noting that the positions depend on the numbering system.

image.alt: Diagram with interior region shaded. Same-side interior pairs highlighted: ∠3 and ∠5 in blue (C-pattern on one side), ∠4 and ∠6 in red (C-pattern on other side). Labels: 'Interior + Same side = Same-Side Interior (Consecutive Interior).' The C-pattern is the visual signature.
image.caption: Step 1: Identify same-side interior angles — interior, same side of transversal, C-pattern.

process_flow.description: Find the corresponding angle that matches one of the same-side interior angles. This connects the same-side pair to a linear pair.
process_flow.mechanism: For ∠4 (at the upper intersection), the corresponding angle at the lower intersection is ∠8. By the Corresponding Angles Postulate: ∠4 ≅ ∠8, so m∠4 = m∠8. This step replaces the measure of ∠4 with the measure of ∠8 at the lower intersection.
process_flow.result: An equality linking one same-side interior angle to an angle at the other intersection.
applications: This places both relevant angle measures at the same intersection.
Now ∠8 and ∠5 are at the same intersection and form a linear pair.

image.alt: ∠4 and ∠8 highlighted as corresponding angles (same position). The Corresponding Angles Postulate cited. An arrow shows m∠4 = m∠8. This transfers the angle measure to the lower intersection.
image.caption: Step 2: Use the Corresponding Angles Postulate to connect ∠4 to ∠8 at the other intersection.

process_flow.process: Apply the Linear Pair Postulate and Substitute
process_flow.description: At the lower intersection, ∠8 and ∠5 form a linear pair, so they are supplementary. Substitute m∠4 for m∠8 to obtain the final sum.
process_flow.mechanism: ∠8 and ∠5 are adjacent angles at the intersection of the transversal and line n. Their non-common sides form a line, so they are a linear pair. By the Linear Pair Postulate: m∠8 + m∠5 = 180°. Since m∠4 = m∠8 (from step 2), substitute: m∠4 + m∠5 = 180°. The same logic applies to the other pair: m∠3 + m∠6 = 180°.
process_flow.result: The same-side interior angles are proven supplementary (sum = 180°).
applications: Complete proof: Corresponding + Linear Pair + Substitution = Same-Side Interior Theorem.

image.alt: At the lower intersection, ∠8 and ∠5 highlighted as a linear pair (supplementary). The equation m∠8 + m∠5 = 180°. Arrow showing m∠4 replacing m∠8: m∠4 + m∠5 = 180°. The C-pattern now fully justified.
image.caption: Step 3: ∠8 and ∠5 are a linear pair (180°). Substitute m∠4 = m∠8 → m∠4 + m∠5 = 180°.

classifications.value: The formal proof of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem in standard format.
characteristics: Given: m ∥ n, t is a transversal.
Statement 3: m∠4 = m∠8 — Definition of Congruent Angles.
Statement 4: ∠8 and ∠5 form a linear pair — Definition (diagram).
Statement 5: m∠8 + m∠5 = 180° — Linear Pair Postulate.
classifications.behavior: This six-step proof is slightly longer than the alternate interior proof because it requires converting congruence to equality and using substitution. It demonstrates the logical connection between corresponding angles, linear pairs, and the supplementary relationship.

examples.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) m ∥ n | Given
2) ∠4 ≅ ∠8 | Corresponding Angles Postulate
3) m∠4 = m∠8 | Definition of Congruent Angles
4) ∠8 and ∠5 form a linear pair | Definition (diagram)
5) m∠8 + m∠5 = 180° | Linear Pair Postulate
6) m∠4 + m∠5 = 180° | Substitution Property

image.alt: A two-column proof with six rows. Left column: statements 1-6. Right column: reasons. The diagram shows the angle chain: ∠4 ≅ ∠8 (corresponding), ∠8+∠5=180° (linear pair), → ∠4+∠5=180° (substitution). Colors for each logical step.
image.caption: Complete two-column proof: six statements establishing the supplementary relationship.

classifications.type: Comparison of All Four Parallel Line Angle Relationships
classifications.value: A summary table showing how same-side interior angles differ from the other three relationships.
characteristics: Corresponding: Same position, different intersections → CONGRUENT
Alternate Interior: Interior, opposite sides → CONGRUENT
Alternate Exterior: Exterior, opposite sides → CONGRUENT
Same-Side Interior: Interior, same side → SUPPLEMENTARY (sum 180°)
Same-side interior is the ONLY relationship that involves a sum rather than equality
classifications.behavior: This distinction is crucial for problem-solving. If you see a C-pattern (same-side interior), use m∠A + m∠B = 180°. If you see a Z-pattern or matching corners, use m∠A = m∠B.

examples.explanation:
Z-pattern or matching corners → set equal.

C-pattern or U-pattern → add to 180°.

This simple visual rule prevents the most common mistake in parallel line problems.

image.alt: A 2×2 table: Rows: Congruent, Supplementary. Columns: Interior, Exterior, Mixed. Corresponding (mixed, ≅), Alternate Interior (interior, ≅), Alternate Exterior (exterior, ≅), Same-Side Interior (interior, 180°). Each cell has a small diagram and the equation.
image.caption: Complete comparison: three ≅ relationships, one 180° relationship. The C-pattern is unique.

image.alt: Complete section summary: the theorem statement, the identification guide (interior + same side = C-pattern), the six-step proof flow, the two-column proof format, and the comparison table of all four relationships.
image.caption: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem overview: identification, proof, and its unique supplementary relationship.

sections.section_title: Using the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem to Find Angle Measures
sections.introduction: Unlike the other parallel line theorems which give direct equalities, the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem gives a sum equation: m∠A + m∠B = 180°. This means finding one angle from the other requires subtraction (180° minus the known angle) rather than simply copying the measure.

introduction_image.alt: Parallel lines with transversal. m∠4 = 65° given. Equation: m∠4 + m∠6 = 180° → 65° + m∠6 = 180° → m∠6 = 115°. The C-pattern connecting ∠4 and ∠6 is highlighted. Both angles labeled with their measures.
introduction_image.caption: Same-side interior angles sum to 180°. Given one, subtract from 180° to find the other.

key_concept.definition: When parallel lines are given, the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem provides the equation m∠3 + m∠5 = 180° and m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°. To find one same-side interior angle given the other, subtract the known measure from 180°. This is different from corresponding or alternate interior angles, where the known measure is simply copied.
key_concept.context: This supplementary relationship is the only one that requires subtraction. Students often mistakenly set same-side interior angles equal, producing an incorrect answer. Recognizing the C-pattern and recalling 'sum = 180°' prevents this error.

image.alt: Diagram with m∠3 = 70°. The theorem applied: m∠3 + m∠5 = 180° → m∠5 = 110°. The C-pattern shown. Contrast with alternate interior: if ∠4=70°, then ∠6=70° (equal, not supplementary). The distinction visually emphasized.
image.caption: Same-side interior = supplementary (subtract from 180°). Alternate interior = congruent (copy the measure).

worked_examples.title: Finding Angles Using Same-Side Interior Angles
worked_examples.problem:
Given: m ∥ n. Find the requested angle measures.

1) m∠4 = 72°. Find m∠6.
2) m∠3 = 5x + 10, m∠5 = 3x + 30. Find x and both angle measures.
3) m∠4 = 2x + 15, m∠5 = 3x − 10. Are these same-side interior angles? If so, find x and the measures.

step_by_step.explanation:
∠4 and ∠6 are same-side interior angles (C-pattern: both interior, same side of transversal).

Since m ∥ n: m∠4 + m∠6 = 180° (Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem).

72° + m∠6 = 180° → m∠6 = 108°.

∠4 (lower-left at top) and ∠5 (upper-left at bottom). Both are on the LEFT side of the transversal. Both are interior. They are on the SAME side → same-side interior angles. YES.

m∠4 + m∠5 = 180°
(2x + 15) + (3x − 10) = 180°
5x + 5 = 180°
5x = 175°
x = 35

m∠4 = 2(35) + 15 = 85°
m∠5 = 3(35) − 10 = 95°
Check: 85° + 95° = 180° ✓.

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) m∠4=72° → m∠6=108° with C-pattern. (2) Algebraic expressions for ∠3 and ∠5, solution x=17.5, angles 97.5° and 82.5°. (3) Algebraic expressions for ∠4 and ∠5, solution x=35, angles 85° and 95°. Each with C-pattern highlighted.
image.caption: Finding measures with the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: subtraction from 180° and algebraic sum equations.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: sum = 180°. Use addition equation (not equality) for algebra.

sections.section_title: The Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem
sections.introduction: The converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem provides the fourth standard method for proving two lines are parallel. If same-side interior angles are supplementary (sum to 180°), the lines must be parallel. This completes the set of four converses—corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior, and same-side interior.

introduction_image.alt: Two lines m and n with transversal t. ∠4 and ∠6 marked with measures 65° and 115° summing to 180°. An arrow points to the conclusion: 'Therefore, m ∥ n.' The converse statement displayed: 'If same-side interior angles are supplementary, then the lines are parallel.'
introduction_image.caption: Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: supplementary → parallel lines.

key_concept.definition: Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: If two lines are cut by a transversal and a pair of same-side interior angles are supplementary (sum to 180°), then the lines are parallel. This is the fourth and final converse for proving parallelism.
key_concept.context: With all four converses established, any angle relationship at a transversal can potentially prove lines are parallel. The choice of which converse to use depends on which angle pair is given or can be proved to have the required relationship.

image.alt: The converse displayed with a diagram. Given: m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°. Prove: m ∥ n. A proof sketch: (1) m∠4 + m∠6 = 180° (Given), (2) m∠8 + m∠6 = 180° (Linear Pair), (3) m∠4 = m∠8 (Algebra/Subtraction), (4) ∠4 ≅ ∠8, and they are corresponding → m ∥ n (Converse of Corresponding Angles Postulate).
image.caption: Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: supplementary same-side interior → parallel lines.

worked_examples.title: Using the Converse to Prove Lines Parallel
worked_examples.problem:
Determine if the lines are parallel based on the given information. Justify using the Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem.

1) m∠3 = 65°, m∠5 = 115°
2) m∠4 = 2x, m∠6 = 3x, and m∠4 + m∠6 = 180° (with x = 36). Prove the lines are parallel.
3) Given: ∠4 and ∠6 are same-side interior angles, and m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°. Prove: m ∥ n.

step_by_step.explanation:
∠3 and ∠5 are same-side interior angles (consecutive interior).

m∠3 + m∠5 = 65° + 115° = 180°. They are supplementary.

By the Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem, the lines are parallel. YES.

With x = 36: m∠4 = 2(36) = 72°, m∠6 = 3(36) = 108°.
72° + 108° = 180° → supplementary.

∠4 and ∠6 are same-side interior angles. Since they are supplementary, the lines are parallel by the Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem.

1) ∠4 and ∠6 are same-side interior angles — Given
2) m∠4 + m∠6 = 180° — Given
3) m ∥ n — Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem

image.alt: Three diagrams: (1) ∠3=65° and ∠5=115° summing to 180°, parallel conclusion. (2) Algebraic expressions with x=36, angles 72° and 108° summing to 180°. (3) Congruence/supplementary marks, formal proof structure.
image.caption: Proving lines parallel using the Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) Yes, parallel (Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem).
2) Yes, parallel (sum = 180°).
3) m ∥ n (Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem).
worked_examples.concept_applied: Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem: supplementary same-side interior angles prove lines are parallel.

sections.section_title: Common Mistakes with Same-Side Interior Angles
sections.introduction: Same-side interior angles present the most common parallel line error: setting them equal instead of adding to 180°. This mistake stems from the fact that the other three angle pair types are all congruent, creating a mental default of 'parallel lines → equal angles' that must be overridden for the C-pattern.

key_concept.definition: The most common error with same-side interior angles is treating them as congruent (setting m∠A = m∠B) instead of supplementary (setting m∠A + m∠B = 180°). Other errors include confusing the C-pattern with the Z-pattern, applying the theorem when lines are not parallel, and misidentifying which pairs are same-side interior.
key_concept.context: This error is so prevalent because three of the four parallel line relationships involve congruence. The C-pattern is the sole exception. Active awareness of this distinction—'C means add to 180°'—is the key to avoiding the error.

image.alt: Four warning panels: (1) Setting same-side interior angles equal (red X) vs. adding to 180° (green check). (2) C-pattern vs Z-pattern confusion. (3) Applying theorem without parallel lines. (4) Misidentifying which angles are same-side interior.
image.caption: Common same-side interior errors: equality instead of sum, pattern confusion, and missing hypothesis.

classifications.type: Setting Same-Side Interior Angles Equal Instead of Adding to 180°
classifications.value: The single most common error in all of parallel line geometry.
characteristics: Error: Writing m∠3 = m∠5 when ∠3 and ∠5 are same-side interior angles
Correct: Same-side interior → SUPPLEMENTARY → m∠3 + m∠5 = 180°
This error produces completely wrong angle measures
classifications.behavior: This error occurs because students internalize 'parallel lines → equal angles' from the more frequently used corresponding and alternate interior theorems. The C-pattern is the exception that must be consciously remembered.

examples.explanation:
Diagram: Parallel lines, transversal. ∠4 = 5x, ∠6 = 4x + 20. ∠4 and ∠6 are same-side interior.

Error: 5x = 4x + 20 → x = 20 → m∠4 = 100°, m∠6 = 100°. (Wrong — they should sum to 180°, not be equal.)

Correct: 5x + (4x + 20) = 180° → 9x + 20 = 180° → 9x = 160° → x ≈ 17.8° → m∠4 = 89°, m∠6 = 91°. Check: 89° + 91° = 180° ✓.

image.alt: Two solution attempts side by side: (1) Incorrect — angles set equal, produces matching 100° angles, sum = 200° ≠ 180°. Red X. (2) Correct — angles added to 180°, produces 89° and 91°, sum = 180°. Green check.
image.caption: Same-side interior: ADD to 180°, never set equal. The C-pattern is supplementary.

classifications.note: Solution: Train yourself to see the C-pattern and think 'sum = 180°.' Before writing any equation, ask: 'Are these same-side interior (C-pattern) or alternate interior (Z-pattern)?' If C → add. If Z → set equal.

image.alt: Summary of common errors with corrected approaches. The equality error prominently featured with both incorrect and correct work shown. C-pattern vs Z-pattern comparison. Decision flowchart: C-pattern? → Sum to 180°. Z-pattern or matching corners? → Set equal.
image.caption: Avoiding same-side interior errors: C-pattern means sum to 180°, always.

sections.introduction: Mastery of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem requires practice identifying C-patterns, setting up sum equations, solving for unknowns, and applying the converse. Work through these problems to build fluency with this unique supplementary relationship.

worked_examples.title: Same-Side Interior Angles Practice
worked_examples.problem:
1) Given: m ∥ n. m∠3 = 68°. Find m∠5 and explain.

2) Given: m ∥ n. m∠4 = 7x + 5, m∠6 = 3x + 25. Find x and both angle measures.

3) Given: m∠3 + m∠5 = 180°. Prove: m ∥ n.

4) Write the complete two-column proof of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem (that m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°).

step_by_step.explanation:
∠3 and ∠5 are same-side interior angles (C-pattern). Since m ∥ n: m∠3 + m∠5 = 180°.

68° + m∠5 = 180° → m∠5 = 112°.

1) m∠3 + m∠5 = 180° — Given
2) ∠3 and ∠5 are same-side interior angles — Definition (from diagram)
3) m ∥ n — Converse of the Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem

Statements | Reasons
1) m ∥ n | Given
2) ∠4 ≅ ∠8 | Corresponding Angles Postulate
3) m∠4 = m∠8 | Definition of Congruent Angles
4) ∠8 and ∠6 form a linear pair | Definition (diagram)
5) m∠8 + m∠6 = 180° | Linear Pair Postulate
6) m∠4 + m∠6 = 180° | Substitution Property

image.alt: Four panels: (1) m∠3=68° → m∠5=112° with C-pattern. (2) Algebraic solution with x=15, angles 110° and 70°. (3) Converse proof. (4) Complete two-column proof of the theorem.
image.caption: Same-side interior angles practice: measures, algebra, converse, and theorem proof.

worked_examples.final_answer:
1) m∠5 = 112°.
2) x = 15, m∠4 = 110°, m∠6 = 70°.
3) m ∥ n (Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem).
4) See step-by-step for complete proof.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem and its converse: sum equations, algebraic solutions, and formal proof structure.

key_terms: Converse of Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem
summary: The Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem is the only parallel line angle relationship that produces supplementary angles rather than congruent ones. Key points: 1) Theorem: If two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, same-side interior angles are supplementary (sum to 180°). Two pairs: m∠3 + m∠5 = 180° and m∠4 + m∠6 = 180°. 2) Visual Identification: Same-side interior angles form a C-pattern or U-pattern—both angles are interior (between the lines) and on the same side of the transversal. 3) Proof: Uses the Corresponding Angles Postulate to connect one same-side interior angle to an angle at the other intersection, then applies the Linear Pair Postulate and Substitution. Six steps in two-column format. 4) Uniqueness: Among the four parallel line angle relationships, three are congruent (corresponding, alternate interior, alternate exterior) and only same-side interior is supplementary. This distinction is critical—the C-pattern always means sum to 180°. 5) Converse: If same-side interior angles are supplementary, the lines are parallel. The fourth and final way to prove parallelism. 6) Common Error: Setting same-side interior angles equal (by habit from the other three relationships) instead of adding to 180°. Remedy: Train yourself to see the C-pattern and think 'sum = 180°.' Before writing any equation, ask whether the angles form a C-pattern or a Z-pattern. C → add; Z → set equal.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Same-Side Interior Angles Theorem' in center. Branches: Statement (parallel lines → same-side interior supplementary, pairs m∠3+m∠5=180°, m∠4+m∠6=180°), Proof (Corresponding Angles Postulate + Linear Pair Postulate + Substitution), Uniqueness (only supplementary relationship of the four types), Converse (supplementary → parallel lines), Visual Pattern (C-pattern, interior + same side), Common Error (setting equal instead of adding to 180°).
review_questions_image.caption: Parallel Lines and Same-Side Interior Angles Review: the unique supplementary relationship, its proof, converse, and common pitfalls.
